content:
4-1 audio multiplexer initiated on May 12, 2020, codename EM22.
Stereo speaker initiated on , codename EM21-A.
   Figure 1 EM21-A and EM22   These two devices were made to enjoy audio contents from multiple analog sound sources. EM22 selects output channels and EM21-A amplifies the selection and outputs them via two built-in loudspeakers, or directly via a 3.5mm panel headphone jack.
Switching between audio channels requires only a simple press on either of the four green buttons on EM22, fairly easy. A built-in buzzer beeps shortly each time a new channel is ready.
4-1 Audio Multiplexer Audio signals reside in the frequency band from 30 to 20kHz. Therefore, a CD4056 4-way electronic multiplexer chip is capable of transferring such signals.
  Figure 2 Diagram of EM22   Photos
 Front panel of EM22 Rear panel of EM22  Stereo Speaker The speaker features a dual 3-watt audio power amplifier IC PAM8403. The amplifier is powered by a 2-cell paralleled lithium battery pack via a USB type-C charging circuitry. The device is quite power efficient, it can last for approximately 7 days per charge.
A front-installed 3.5mm is employed to switch audio output from built-in loudspeakers to external headphones or other audio devices. In this scenario, internal speakers become mute.
Mute feature is accomplished through the mechanic structures of internal metal contacts in a 5-pin 3.5mm audio socket, though the PAM8403 chip provides such mute function per se.   Figure 3 Diagram of EM21-A   PAM8406 as the PA is hardwired as in the following sketch.
  Figure 4 Schematic of PAM8403   Photos
 Front panel of EM21-A Rear panel of EM21-A Top view  Stackable Version of EM22-α The α version of EM22 supports more features which were abandoned in the final version.
 Touch buttons USB MUX and hub Onboard battery charger and protector  Photos
 MUX board of EM22-α Stacked circuit boards of EM22-α (Control board, USB hub board, audio MUX board)  Power-line Distortion Consideration When processing audio sources that are powered directly from wall sockets (110V/60Hz or 220V/50Hz), battery-powered EM21-A and EM22 suffer from a so-called power-line distortion at corresponding frequency. This is also known as harmonic distortion of the AC power line, where a sinusoidal noise coupled from power lines into audio circuitry, disturbs fine audio signals. Details on the cause of power line distortion can be found in Electrical Engineering Portal and GC Audio.
One possible solution is to use a notch filter, which attenuates signal tremendously at the target frequency as shown (50Hz). Signal at 50Hz is reduced to under -60dB, meaning it is reduced more than 1000 times. Therefore, disturbing noise is almost eliminated. However, this interrupts desired input at the target frequency, although no fine audio generate a 50Hz or 60Hz sound.
  Figure 5 Notch filter at 50Hz
summary:
4-1 audio multiplexer initiated on May 12, 2020, codename EM22.
Stereo speaker initiated on , codename EM21-A.
   Figure 1 EM21-A and EM22   These two devices were made to enjoy audio contents from multiple analog sound sources. EM22 selects output channels and EM21-A amplifies the selection and outputs them via two built-in loudspeakers, or directly via a 3.5mm panel headphone jack.
Switching between audio channels requires only a simple press on either of the four green buttons on EM22, fairly easy.
title: 4-1 Audio MUX and Stereo Speaker

content:
codename EM10
 Lithium battery is one of the most commonly used power sources in amateur projects, especially in handheld devices. Under this scenario, an efficient full-featured yet low-profile battery charger which can be easily and transparently embedded is the ultimate requirement for a part selection consideration.
TP4056 from Top Power ASIC® is such a candidate. This tiny chip is an all-in-one, full featured, integrated solution supporting up to 1 ampere current lithium battery charger and costs less than $0.5 per piece. TP4056 has a complete built-in battery protecting circuitry including short circuit, over current, over/under voltage, over temperature.
The chip is delivered in a plastic SOP-8 package in the size of 5×6×2mm, smaller than a green bean. With the reasonably small package, amateur engineers are capable of either surface mount it on to a printed circuit board (PCB) or hand-wire the chip to build their prototypes.
  Figure.1 Small outline package (SOP-8)   Back to TP4056. Figure 2 shows a full featured application of the chip with two LED indicators for charging and standby status and an NTC sensor circuitry for battery temperature detection.
  Figure.2 Application of TP4056 (provided by Top Power ASIC®)   What is important is the $R_\text{prog}$ resistor which programs the maximum charging current of TP4056. Therefore, a minimal configuration can be established with only $R_\text{prog}$ and the TP4056 per se. Thermal sensoring feature is disabled while the internal 140°C threshold still functions. All decoupling capacitors can be removed in certain scenarios where there is a large tolerance as those in figure 3.
  Figure.3 Minimal configuration of TP4056, hand-wired   Finally, with a custom PCB, we are able to build an easy-to-use battery charger module with onboard charging status indicators in a 0.1" pitch TO-220 package size (15×10×3mm).
  Figure.4 A full-featured 3-pin EM10 comparing to a TO-220 packaged 7805 regulator
summary:
codename EM10
 Lithium battery is one of the most commonly used power sources in amateur projects, especially in handheld devices. Under this scenario, an efficient full-featured yet low-profile battery charger which can be easily and transparently embedded is the ultimate requirement for a part selection consideration.
TP4056 from Top Power ASIC® is such a candidate. This tiny chip is an all-in-one, full featured, integrated solution supporting up to 1 ampere current lithium battery charger and costs less than $0.
title: 1A Lithium Battery Charger

content:
Ebay  is an amazing e-commercial website containing huge amounts of goods trading on it. Thanks to Python, it is possible to extract every single detail of items on Ebay.
The requests.get() function can only fetch statically served pages, which means any late-binding dynamically generated pages may suffer a unsuccessful retrieval. Generate URL with Search Keywords To crawl a website, a correct resource location is required to fetch web pages containing desired contents. In this Ebay example, a URL with a keyword in the path parameter is used to obtain pages returned by the server based on the keywords to be searched.
https://www.ebay.com/sch/i.html?_nkw=web+crawler This shall return a page like this.
  Figure.1 Ebay search page with keywords web crawler   Now, a function wrapping keyword strings into a target URL is able to be established.
def buildUrl(baseUrl, param, keyword): if (keyword): return "{}?{}={}".format(baseUrl, param, keyword.replace(" ", "+")) else: return baseUrl url = buildUrl( "https://www.ebay.com/sch/i.html", "_nkw", "web crawler" ) https://www.ebay.com/sch/i.html?_nkw=web+crawler Analyze Page Structures One of the preparations to be done is to analyze structures of the pages beforehand. This step is extremely essential for all web crawlers, no matter which crawling library is employed.
With the correct URL, it is able to fetch all HTML source code from that search result. Code to retrieve web pages can be found in the previous introduction.
Of the keywords web crawler fetched on 5th. February 2021, the content is as follows.
<!DOCTYPE html><!--[if IE 9]><html class="ie9 srp-ds6 srp-ds6-phase3" lang="en"><![endif]--><!--[if gt IE 9]><!--><html class="srp-ds6 srp-ds6-phase3" lang="en"><!--<![endif]--><!--M#s0-1--><noscript class=x-page-config id=s0-1></noscript><!--M/--><head><meta http-equiv=X-UA-Compatible content=IE=edge><script>"use strict";if(window.PerformanceObserver&&performance&&performance.mark&&performance.getEntriesByName){window.SRP=window.SRP||{};var paintObserver=new window.PerformanceObserver(function(e){var r=e.getEntries();r.sort(function(e,r){return e.startTime-r.startTime});var n=r[1].startTime;window.SRP.TTI_TIMER={lastInteractiveWindow:n};var t=new window.PerformanceObserver(function(e){for(var r=e.getEntries(),i=0,a=r.length;i<a;i++)r[i].startTime-n>=5e3&&(window.SRP.TTI_TIMER.timeToInteract=n,t.disconnect()),n=r[i].startTime+r[i].duration,window.SRP.TTI_TIMER.lastInteractiveWindow=n}); ... It is easy to find the sections that contain product information with development tools of the web browser, hover the cursor on that area, right click to choose “inspect element” or something alike, the source code that renders this element with be emphasized with a shadow area in the debug panel. Here is an example of a single item area on ebay, referenced with a black arrow between source code and its rendered page element.
  Figure.2 Product item code area indicated in Safari’s development tool   An item is wrapped in an <li> tag with a class list of s-item and other decorative classes. Digging into this li.s-item node, all essential information of an item, in this case, a toy crawler car, is ready to be extracted.
  Figure.3 Item information and position in source code    Full-sized code analysis images are available: cover, link, title, and price.
 Take the code segment that contains product title for example. The remotely fetched source code is as follows.
<a data-track='{"eventFamily":"LST","eventAction":"ACTN","actionKind":"NAVSRC","actionKinds":["NAVSRC"],"operationId":"2351460","flushImmediately":false,"eventProperty":{"moduledtl":"mi:1686|iid:1|li:7400|luid:1|scen:Listings","parentrq":"70f7fcc81770aaf49ff691edfffd4584","pageci":"ea899f32-677e-11eb-949d-b22c42c4f639"}}' _sp="p2351460.m1686.l7400" class="s-item__link" href="https://www.ebay.com/itm/Scx24-C10-web-Flatbed-Axial-Crawler-1-24-Scale-Accessories-Cooler-Jerry-Can/353374483646?hash=item5246c2b4be:g:rDIAAOSwippf-i9U"> <h3 class="s-item__title"> Scx24 C10 web Flatbed Axial Crawler 1/24 Scale Accessories Cooler Jerry Can </h3> </a> It is easy to write a regular expression to extract the literal book title. Be aware of that the source text may not be always well-formatted, thus conditional operators "? and .*? are used to guarantee the expression matches in as many as potential situations.
<h3 class="?s-item__title.*?"?>(.*?)<\/h3> Which matches book title in the first matching group.
import re pattern = '<h3 class="?s-item__title.*?"?>(.*?)<\/h3>' match = re.search(pattern, response.text) print(match.group(1)) Web Scraping with Python: Collecting More Data from the Modern Web With such successful extraction, further information of the items is now on the way. Try these regular expressions as proposed. (Wrapped in a dictionary.)
expression = { 'title': '<h3 class="?s-item__title.*?"?>(.*?)<\/h3>', 'cover': '<img class="?s-item__image-img"?.*?src="?([^"]*?)"?\s', 'price': '<span class="?s-item__price"?>(.*?)<\/span>' } A more efficient approach is to combine these scattered expressions into one single huge expression with groups for all parameters. This will tremendously reduce consumption in searching for regular expression patterns.
pattern = '<img class="?s-item__image-img"?.*?src="?([^"]*?)"?\s.*?<h3 class="?s-item__title.*?"?>(.*?)<\/h3>.*?<span class="?s-item__price"?>(.*?)<\/span>' expressions = { 'title': 2, 'cover': 1, 'price': 3 } The pattern has 3 groups which contains sections of demanding information.
# item code block separation segments = response.text.split('<li class="s-item') items = [] # search each block with the pattern for segment in segments: m = re.search(pattern, segment) # found, build data object if m: temp = {} for key in exp.keys(): temp[key] = m.group(exp[key]) # store items.append(temp) print(items) [{ 'title': 'Scx24 C10 web Flatbed Axial ... Cooler Jerry Can', 'cover': 'https://i.ebayimg.com/.../s-l225.jpg', 'price': '$30.00' }, { 'title': 'FAT QUARTER SPIDERMAN FABRIC WEB CRAWLER ... COTTON FQ', 'cover': 'https://i.ebayimg.com/.../s-l225.jpg', 'price': '$3.95' }, { ... Fantastic! Now, feel free to start building your own web crawlers!
Structural Solutions Since regular expression is so complicated, time-consuming and scenario-dependent, even the most experienced masters may sometimes feel it arduous to build proper expressions. Based on the fact of tree-structured DOM that elements of an HTML file are organized, several decent DOM orientated content analyzers/parsers could help to reduce the difficulty and complexity in data extraction.
XPath and JSONPath, as the names suggest, are libraries for discovery nodes through specified paths in XML and JSON files, respectively. Beautiful Soup is another powerful “all-in-one” parser for HTML/XML content extraction, depending on the parsers selection.
Let’s try the Ebay example again. This time, the Beautiful Soup library will be used instead.
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup bs = BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser") As analyzed in figure 3, information of an item is represented by contents contained in DOM nodes in HTML source. Skimming through the DOM, we will get a class-marked structure of item information as in figure 4.
  Figure.4 Item information DOM tree (partial)   Regardless of deeply nested in the source, it is possible to instantly extract parts (nodes) that contains information we need using the Beautiful Soup library. In the following example, an html.parser, which is the Python’s standard parser, is assigned to the instance to cope with HTML source inputs.
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup bs = BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser") bs.title.string web crawler | eBay With the power of DOM, we are able to search in parsed DOM tree by simply passing tag names and classes as parameters to the .find() function.
bs.find("h3", class_ = "s-item__title").string Scx24 C10 web Flatbed Axial Crawler 1/24 Scale Accessories Cooler Jerry Can Similarly, with maps done in figure 4, we can now extract information of the product with Beautiful Soup.
title = bs.find("h3", class_ = "s-item__title").string cover = bs.find("img", class_ = "s-item__image-img").get("src") price = bs.find("span", class_ = "s-item__price").string print({"title": title, "cover": cover, "price": price}) { 'title': 'Scx24 C10 web Flatbed Axial Crawler ... Cooler Jerry Can', 'cover': 'https://i.ebayimg.com/thumbs/images/.../s-l225.jpg', 'price': '$30.00' } Multiple Item Extraction Beautiful Soup provides a .find_all() function that behaves as a multi-target version of .find(). With such function, it is simple to accomplish what has been done to the whole HTML source with regular expressions in section Analyze Page Structures. In other words, we are about to extract all items on the page simultaneously.
titles = bs.find_all("h3", class_ = "s-item__title") covers = bs.find_all("img", class_ = "s-item__image-img") prices = bs.find_all("span", class_ = "s-item__price") items = [] for i in range(1, len(titles)): item = { "title": titles[i].string, "cover": covers[i].get("src"), "price": prices[i].string } items.append(item) print(items) [{ 'title': 'FAT QUARTER SPIDERMAN FABRIC ... COTTON FQ', 'cover': 'https://i.ebayimg.com/thumbs/.../s-l225.jpg', 'price': '$3.95' }, { 'title': 'SPIDERMAN FABRIC WEB CRAWLER ... THE YARD', 'cover': 'https://i.ebayimg.com/thumbs/.../s-l225.jpg', 'price': '$12.99' }, { ... The first three highlighted lines were rewritten from that in the previous section with .find_all() to hold a collection of information of its kind. Since every item has all three pieces of information, lengths of titles, covers, and prices are guaranteed to be the same. A loop of length of titles starting from 1 in line 5 is employed to iterate through all these collections, building every item object and store such item in a new items list for future use.
summary:
Ebay  is an amazing e-commercial website containing huge amounts of goods trading on it. Thanks to Python, it is possible to extract every single detail of items on Ebay.
The requests.get() function can only fetch statically served pages, which means any late-binding dynamically generated pages may suffer a unsuccessful retrieval. Generate URL with Search Keywords To crawl a website, a correct resource location is required to fetch web pages containing desired contents.
title: ebay Item Extractor

content:
Web crawlers, usually known as web spiders, are automated data samplers and remote content fetchers. These programmed agents retrieve web contents and store them for future analytic usage. A typical crawler loads web pages from the internet, abstracts contents of specified fields in page HTML sources, and constructs these data into a JSON object or other formats of storage. In this scenario, the crawler substitutes web browsers operated by human clients.
  Figure.1 Web crawler in the internet   As shown in figure 1, a web crawler disguises the role of a human-controlled web browser. Instead of surfing via the browser, the web crawler obtains a full access to the internet, and, consequentially, presses all data retrieved from the cloud into storages.
Example Web Crawler A simplest web crawler can be implemented with a single feature, which is to retrieve web data from the internet. No future data processors or analyzers attached except a simple regular expression extractor for the page title. One of the most commonly used libraries for this job is requests.
Note that a request header, which can be found in development tools of all modern web browsers, must be employed to disguise this web crawler as a regular web browser client, or the anti-crawling server may return a 418 error instead of demanding web pages.
Let’s take Amazon’s website  for example, sample response will probably resemble the following fragment (crawled on 3rd Feb. 2021):
import requests url = "https://www.amazon.com" header = { "user-agent": "Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X ... Safari/605.1.15" } # retrieve content at given location with the request header response = requests.get(url, headers = header) print(response.text) <!doctype html><html lang="en-us" class="a-no-js" data-19ax5a9jf="dingo"><!-- sp:feature:head-start --> <head><script>var aPageStart = (new Date()).getTime();</script><meta charset="utf-8"/> <script type='text/javascript'>var ue_t0=ue_t0||+new Date();</script> <!-- sp:feature:cs-optimization --> ... The output via .text property is automatically detected for its encoding. However, a manually specified encoding can be introduced by assigning encoding names to the .encoding property. You should also aware of that these contents are plain text bytes, not structured data objects even if the text indeed is of an HTML source.
response = requests.get(...) response.encoding = "utf-8" # mandatory encoding specification ... So far, we’ve succeeded downloading, or more popularly, crawling contents from the internet. Further fancy procedures are now able to be applied on the data stored in response.text.
Data Extraction It is obvious that crawling web pages from the internet makes the web crawler no more than a dumb browser. Data hidden underneath the bundle of HTML source text shall be extracted and well-organized for the next phase of a real practical web crawler.
Plenty of data extractors are available out there, depending on the format of content, e.g. Beautiful Soup, XPath, JSONPath, etc. Almost every extractor library utilizes the solo powerful yet complicated tool - regular expressions, or RegEx - to a certain extent.
Python provides the ability of manipulating regular expressions through a library named re. In this section, I am implementing a simple extractor with the re library, directly draw desired content from the whole. Fundamental concepts and details on regular expressions can be googled and will not be discussed in this article.
While implementing extractors with re, the desired data and its wrapping structures shall be studied ahead. In this case, I’m going to find the title of what’s retrieved, regardless of the source of web pages. Since every page claims its title in a <title> tag in the head section, it is quite convenient to build the target regular expression with group matches.
<title>(.*?)</title> A non-greedy matching group (.*?) is employed to extract the desired text itself without its wrapper. Therefore, the Python code of such extractor will be:
import re pattern = "<title>(.*?)</title>" match = re.search(pattern, response.text) print(match.group(1)) Amazon.com: Online Shopping for Electronics, Apparel, Computers, Books, DVDs & more And the title of the page crawled, from the previous Amazon’s homepage, is unveiled.
Definitely, text-based regular expressions are arduous to master. Some DOM-based1 solutions, as mentioned beforehand, could be a better and more effective choice for new programmers.
Data Storage and Analysis After everything is ready extracted, under most circumstances, the data is stored in either structured data objects/variables, or in a designated database. Specified analysis will then be carried out over these data, to study deepened characteristics or patterns of the original data source. The entire “fetch-extract-store-analyze” procedure makes the Python codes a “web crawler”.
  Document Object Model, the tree-shaped data structure of HTML. ↩︎
summary: Web crawlers, usually known as web spiders, are automated data samplers and remote content fetchers. These programmed agents retrieve web contents and store them for future analytic usage. A typical crawler loads web pages from the internet, abstracts contents of specified fields in page HTML sources, and constructs these data into a JSON object or other formats of storage. In this scenario, the crawler substitutes web browsers operated by human clients.
title: Web Crawler Introduction

content:
Offers are reception passports authorized by universities. They are tickets to your future success. A conditional offer, as the name suggests, is made to those partially qualified candidates who lack some of the requirements at the time of application. If you are made a conditional offer, this means you’ve got the potential acceptance from professors but subject to finish tasks listed in the offer.
Congrats to you! You are at the front gate of your goal. All you have to do is to meet unachieved requirements, which are often language qualifications, recommendation letters, etc., relatively simple and generic.
Recommendations have some potential negative impacts under certain circumstances. Read more in Quora Q&A: Letters of Recommendation, a Unfair Aspect of Admission Note that only some European universities and former British countries such as Singapore and Australia offers such conditional acceptance, while northern America universities, especially those in the United States usually do not.
Real Sample Conditional Offer Here is a sample conditional offers I have obtained from the University of Sydney for the master degree course of data science.
  Figure.1 First page of a conditional offer from the University of Sydney, privacy contents are inked   Along with this offer is the list of requirements (conditions) you have to meet. In this case, there is one condition asking the candidate to upload an evidence of his/her English language proficiency.
  Figure.2 Condition list of the offer from the University of Sydney   Actually, I applied for this course before the Covid-19 pandemic and obtained such a conditional offer in 2019. The one in the screenshot is a second renewed notification copy of the course seat. This is why I congratulate to all conditional offer holders: universities that issue such offers will mark you, a qualified client, and will not give up on you. They don’t want to lose a potential student, you, that they’d notify you frequently. From my experience, I receive emails from the University of Sydney almost every week reminding me of completing the deal.
List of Universities Easy to Apply Of course, by the word easy I mean relatively easy. You’ll still have to be above the bottom lines of their requirements.
 All Australian universities The Russell Group universities Second-tier universities in the UK Universities in Hong Kong, China  More The world’s finest Universities of Cambridge and Oxford issue such conditional offers as well. Are you qualified of their extreme entrance standards? Good luck!
summary:
Offers are reception passports authorized by universities. They are tickets to your future success. A conditional offer, as the name suggests, is made to those partially qualified candidates who lack some of the requirements at the time of application. If you are made a conditional offer, this means you’ve got the potential acceptance from professors but subject to finish tasks listed in the offer.
Congrats to you! You are at the front gate of your goal.
title: Conditional Offers from Universities

content:
Initiated on May 12, 2012, codename EMP1.
 Content of this article is part of a patent pending project, and is protected by patent laws and acts. Reusing it in any forms is considered illegal prior to the patent is issued. Random number generator, or RNG, is one of the most fundamental ingredients of all computers and programming languages, in the form of electronic or software modules. RNGs were invented long before mechanical or electronic computers before there were symbols for writing numbers.
Ancient civilizations used flipping coins or rolling dices to generate independent random numbers. The most antique dices found in Iraq and Iran were made 5,000 years ago. They were also popular in India and China since 2000 BC (Diro, 2017). Modern RNGs are equipped often with a uniformly distributed white noise generator as the source of randomness, which, as a characteristic, provides extremely discrete outputs.
Under most circumstances, this is the exact requirement for RNGs. But, yes there is a but, when simulating continuous natural measurements, such as wind speed, water level, temperature, etc., the uniformity becomes less important, or even an impediment. In this case, we will need a smoother RNG, which introduces my project, a natural-imitating random number generator, the NiRNG. By the phrase “natural imitating”, continuous measurements are concerned.
Continuity of Measurements in Nature Values of measurements relating to nature, temperature readouts on a thermometer, for example, do not change discretely. Say, it is 80℉ now, we don’t expect it be 100℉ in the next second, even if heaters were turned on. These values always change gradually, continuously. However, all random number generator outputs vary extremely quickly, as getting rid of the bias of a random bit sequence is one of the primary tasks of designing RNGs. No next values can be inferred from previous ones. Low correlation guarantees randomness of RNGs, sacrificing continuity of values.
Of course there is quite a chance to guess outputs. $v=v_0\pm\tau p$, where $v_0$ is the latest output, $\tau$ is the increment range size, and $p$ is a RNG produced randomized factor.
Uniformly Distributed RNs vs. Natural-Imitating RNs When we talk about natural-imitating stuffs, we intend to reproduce real world behaviors in our desired environments, or at least, to simulate natural phenomenons. With such a great goal, some foundations must be set, one of which is the natural-imitating random number generator.
Figure 1 presents a visible comparison of two random number sequences generated by a uniformly distributed RNG (black — ) and a NiRNG (red — ). The chart explains the main purpose of the NiRNG: generate random numbers in a relatively continuous manner. The NiRNG data seems much smoother than that of the RNG with a bit of jitters and a recognizable trend in certain neighborhoods while its rival provides wild sudden changes from the very bottom up to the ceiling. Unfortunately, the messed RNG solution is the choice of quite a lot of amateurs and noob engineers, giving gauge hands and meter indicators their most horrified nightmares.
  Figure 1 Uniformly distributed random numbers (BLK) vs. natural-imitating random numbers (RED)   As shown, differences $\delta_k$ between two adjacent values in the NRNG sequence is highly restricted in a small amount. For this particular test data set, the correlation coefficient of RNG $r_\text{RNG}=0.005$, while $r_\text{NRNG}=0.91$, indicating a very high degree of correlation of adjacent data.
Despite of the high bias of the random bits, resulting output data of the NiRNG solution provides a local relatively high randomness in the neighborhood of a bandwidth defined by range of incrementation.
Design of NiRNG   Figure 2 Flow chart of NiRNG (simplified)   Figure 2 shows the flow diagram. Four ranges are employed in this design:
 regular range increment range primary nominal range secondary nominal range  The later two are elected internally by the initial value of output as a final nominal range. A short sequence of minor jitters is appended to the output as spikes.
Take the example in the last section, fig.3 shows its nominal range (elected from primary and secondary nominal ranges) between blue horizontal lines.
  Figure 3 Nominal range of a sequence of NiRNG values   Any output value is based on its prior predecessor. A random increment is generated each time NiRNG outputs and is accumulated onto the previous value. In fact, the NRNG generates random numbers in a much narrower band where randomness is limited, but former output information is kept to guarantee that final output (at any step) be relatively continuous, which makes the most distinction from what ordinary RNGs generate.
Software Implementation Embracing the power of R: This implementation of NiRNG is writtin in R language, one of the most popular statistical programming languages. Core NiRNG algorithm consists of three main procedures: a) determine actual nominal range, b) generate randomized spikes, and c) accumulate random increment onto current value. As implemented in R as follows.
# A simplified approach of NiRNG # 8 Jan. '21 Greg Conmajia if (weight.nominal.switch > 0) if (value >= min.nominal && value <= max.nominal) if (rand() <= weight.nominal.switch) if (min.nominal == min.nominal.primary && max.nominal == max.nominal.primary) { min.nominal <- min.nominal.secondary max.nominal <- max.nominal.secondary } else { min.nominal <- min.nominal.primary max.nominal <- max.nominal.primary } if (weight.spike > 0) if (rand() <= weight.spike) length.spike.remain <- length.spike if (length.spike.remain == 0) { if (rand() > weight.direction) direction <- direction * -1 if (value < min.nominal) if (rand() < weight.nominal) direction <- 1 if (value >= max.nominal) if (rand() < weight.nominal) direction <- -1 } else length.spike.remain <- length.spike.remain - 1 if (value <= minimum) direction <- 1 if (value >= maximum) direction <- -1 value <- value + direction * rand(min.increment * 100, max.increment * 100) / 100 value <- max(value, minimum) value <- min(value, maximum) Above code generates a single numeric output each time, based on given parameters and the noise source rand() could be any type of random number sources, which, in this case, is a function of runif.
# Generates a single random number rand <- function (min = 0, max = 100) { return(runif(1, min = min, max = max)) } Extended Implementation With the use of true noise source hardware, we are enabled to implement NiRNG in the physical world. Noise diodes, heated electron tubes are appropriate cost effective candidates of such sources for homemade projects.
References Diro, et al. 2017. History of uniform random number generation. Proceedings of the 2017 Winter Simulation Conference.
summary:
Initiated on May 12, 2012, codename EMP1.
 Content of this article is part of a patent pending project, and is protected by patent laws and acts. Reusing it in any forms is considered illegal prior to the patent is issued. Random number generator, or RNG, is one of the most fundamental ingredients of all computers and programming languages, in the form of electronic or software modules. RNGs were invented long before mechanical or electronic computers before there were symbols for writing numbers.
title: Natural-Imitating Random Number Generator

content:
Aren’t letters of recommendation an inherently unfair aspect of the college admission process? Original Quora page 
Samuel O. Alfred, Lead Administrator, Computational Mechanics and Artificial Intelligence
Your question is a tough one but I deeply share in your sentiments.
What goes on behind the scene before a recommendation letter gets to the Admission committee is usually undetected by the admissions committee. Though a letter not written by a Professor can be detected, it is not usually the case.(If you are interviewed before a decision is made, closely study the questions you will be asked. They reveal alot).
It takes a great level of perusal to know that a Professor didn’t write the letter. Sometimes, students write the letters and their Professors edit it for use. Ultimately, all these defeat the purpose.
Another worrying aspect is when members of the Admission committee tend to grade applicants by the popularity or ranking of their schools. They tend to favour letters from such schools in that regard. If by a stroke of luck your supervisor is known to someone on the committee or the Professor checking your application package , the odds might be in your favour. There are many good researchers but not all are as popular as the Professor Stingleys (Economics, Columbia University) or the Professor Muellers (Physics,UCLA,Berkeley) of this world.
Even though your Professor has agreed to write a letter for you, he might just open his desktop folder of recommendation letters and edit one on your behalf. I mean, that doesn’t serve the purpose ( I know Professors are really busy but still, you’re unique so you shouldn’t use the same letter as others!). A generic letter hurts a student’s chances. Graduate school admission committee have what they look for. For PhD applications, Professors are looking for best fits into their lab. That’s why you don’t compare you chances of success with someone else’s profile. You can only do that if you have on good authority that, for instance, GRE scores were used to filter applicants. However, most often that nought , you don’t get that information.
These factors are worrisome about the use of recommendation letters. However, this practice has been on for long and I’m not sure it will fade away soon.
Nevertheless, since applications are assessed on a holistic basis, you have little to worry about. A captivating statement of purpose backed up with a good GPA and test score can make things work out well for you.
Saebyukban Phil
I agree with you completely.
The biggest problem obviously being corruption. In countries like S.Korea, LoRs are highly illegal due to the fact that they naturally bring about favoritism and corruption. So, no matter how talented or smart you are, the opportunity will likely go to the prof’s favorite big-mouth brown-nosers. I see too many unqualified people getting what they want through LoRs in today’s society. And speaking of corruption, LoRs give the profs unethical amount of power over students because it is such a subjective domain, which may lead to exploitation of these young people.
How well do these profs know their students, anyway? A few meetings about your projects? A few small talks over coffee breaks? Maybe a deep talk over a dinner if you are very very keen. If you think about it, even the biggest suck-ups don’t really get to know the prof and vice-versa. So, how unnatural is it for them to judge someone over these few moments?
In my opinion, our society have got to get rid of this subjectivity from admission and hiring.
summary:
Aren’t letters of recommendation an inherently unfair aspect of the college admission process? Original Quora page 
Samuel O. Alfred, Lead Administrator, Computational Mechanics and Artificial Intelligence
Your question is a tough one but I deeply share in your sentiments.
What goes on behind the scene before a recommendation letter gets to the Admission committee is usually undetected by the admissions committee. Though a letter not written by a Professor can be detected, it is not usually the case.
title: Quora Q&A: Letters of Recommendation, a Unfair Aspect of Admission

content:
Length and Text 2 pages at most. Be concise, organized, free of errors. Tailor to particular institution.
Never provide a generic, lengthy biography book to your application. Professors of the admission council have no such leisure hours studying your detailed story. They, or their assistants, will quickly glance through hundreds of application materials in a couple of days. Therefore, you will have to catch their sights by using some accurate remarkable keywords in your PS. Do not make any grammatic or spelling errors which will only downgrade assessment score of your application.
Main Idea Express your interests (match) and ambitions (passion and motivation) to the program. Demonstrate possible contributions you will make.
 Interests. Why you apply, how are you match. Converge with current research. Abilities, experience. How can you complete. Ambitions. How will you contribute.  Show your value and convince the admission board. Applying for college programs is a bilateral selection process. You hunt for a potential degree or certificate, the universities seek for a benefitial client and able researcher. That is the reason you are been assessed.
Important  Evidence to abilities to support future study/research. Alignment to professors and programs. Familiarities with university’s current research. Knowledge background.  As mentioned, you will need to demonstrate your qualifications to meet the requirements. Personal stories do little help to this. Point is that to notify the university about eligibilities of yours.
Primary Topics  Interests and related talents, abilities. Experience to support study, breifly detail it. Articulate knowledge background and its stimulation. Future goals. Ph.D, subdisciplines interested. Research interests, agenda. Identify one or two in the subfield. How are you match? Align research interests with professors and programs.  Secondary Topics To support future study, completing the program.
 Example of attributes, qualities. Ability to develop ideas, work independency.  Avoid DO NOT narrate your accomplishments.
Unless you are a mature successful candidate, never attempt to empathize the admission board with your shallow life/study/internship experiences.
summary:
Length and Text 2 pages at most. Be concise, organized, free of errors. Tailor to particular institution.
Never provide a generic, lengthy biography book to your application. Professors of the admission council have no such leisure hours studying your detailed story. They, or their assistants, will quickly glance through hundreds of application materials in a couple of days. Therefore, you will have to catch their sights by using some accurate remarkable keywords in your PS.
title: Essentials for Personal Statements
